news_summary: Innovative conservation efforts are transforming ocean plastic into roads, reintroducing lost species, exploring legal protections for natural habitats, and successfully repopulating rare butterflies.
news_summary_fr: Des initiatives innovantes en matière de préservation permettent de transformer les déchets plastiques océaniques en routes, de réintroduire des espèces disparues, d'étudier les mesures juridiques de protection des habitats naturels et de réussir à repeupler des populations de papillons rares.
news_summary_es: Las iniciativas innovadoras de conservación están transformando el plástico oceánico en carreteras, reintroduciendo especies desaparecidas, estudiando medidas legales para la protección de los hábitats naturales y repoblando con éxito poblaciones de mariposas raras.

articles.title: Hawaii is turning ocean plastic and fishing nets into roads
articles.summary: Hawaii researchers are giving old fishing nets and recycled plastic a second life by mixing them into asphalt roads. Early tests found these roads didn't release more plastic particles than standard pavement, with tire wear overwhelming any plastic signal from the recycled material. If future studies confirm the roads are durable, the technology could help tackle both marine pollution and overflowing landfills.
articles.content:
Hawaii faces a growing plastic waste challenge. Recycling on the islands is expensive and difficult, and large amounts of marine debris continue to wash ashore or remain in surrounding waters. Now, researchers are exploring an innovative solution by turning discarded fishing nets and household plastic waste into asphalt for roads. Early results suggest the approach could provide a practical new destination for plastics that might otherwise end up in landfills or the ocean.

Jeremy Axworthy, a researcher at the Center for Marine Debris Research (CMDR) at Hawaiʻi Pacific University, presented the findings at the spring meeting of the American Chemical Society (ACS).

"This work investigates whether it's responsible to use recycled plastics in Hawaii's roads," shares Axworthy. "By reusing plastic waste that is already in Hawaii, we can reduce the environmental and economic impacts of transporting waste plastics from the islands, incinerating it or dumping it in Hawaii's overflowing landfills."

Why Hawaii Is Testing Recycled Plastic Roads

Since 2020, most roads in Hawaii have been built using polymer-modified asphalt (PMA), which is designed to improve strength and durability. Compared with conventional asphalt, PMA is more flexible and better able to resist cracking, rutting, and water damage, making it well suited to Hawaii's tropical climate.

To make PMA, pellets of styrene-butadiene-styrene (SBS; a type of copolymer) are melted into a sticky petroleum-based asphalt binder. That binder is then mixed with heated aggregates (rocks and sand), coating the material before it is laid as pavement.

Researchers wondered whether some of the virgin polymer could be replaced with discarded plastics. They also wanted to know whether roads made with recycled plastics would perform well and whether they might release microplastics or other chemicals into the environment. Those questions led the Hawaii Department of Transportation (HDOT) to partner with environmental chemist Jennifer Lynch, director of CMDR and leader of the research team.

Recycling Fishing Nets Into Asphalt

HDOT asked Lynch's team to tackle two key tasks. The first was to supply abandoned fishing nets collected from Hawaii's waters for use in experimental recycled plastic asphalt.

"Foreign plastic derelict fishing gear is the largest contributor of Hawaii's marine debris problem," shares Lynch. "To date, CMDR's Bounty Project, which pays a financial reward to licensed commercial fishers for marine debris removal, has removed 84 tons of large, derelict fishing gear from the Pacific Ocean."

The second goal was to determine whether pavement made with recycled plastic released more microplastics than standard SBS-modified asphalt.

"CMDR's laboratory is equipped with state-of-the-art chemical instrumentation for quantifying and characterizing microplastics in environmental samples," explains Lynch. "This capability is incredibly unique and impactful, especially when coupled to our marine debris-removal project and our mission to recycle the debris into long-term, locally necessary infrastructure products."

After a U.S. company processed the recovered plastics into materials suitable for asphalt production, HDOT moved the project into the real world. A local paving company resurfaced sections of a residential street on Oahu using three different asphalt mixtures: one with standard SBS, one containing recycled polyethylene from Honolulu's residential recycling program, and one made with polyethylene recovered from discarded fishing nets.

About 11 months later, Lynch's team returned to collect road dust from each section so they could measure any microplastic released into the surrounding environment.

Measuring Microplastic Shedding

The scientists separated different types of polymers from the road dust, including microplastics, larger plastic fragments, and tire rubber. They then used pyrolysis gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (Py-GC-MS) to determine where the materials came from. The analysis identified styrene and butadiene from standard PMA, polyethylene from recycled plastic and fishing net pavements, and isoprene and butadiene rubber from vehicle tires.

Early findings showed that pavement containing recycled polyethylene did not release more polymers than conventional SBS pavement. The same pattern appeared in laboratory performance testing and in simulated stormwater collected from the experimental road sections.

Although researchers detected microplastic-sized particles, only a very small number were identified as polyethylene, regardless of which pavement type they came from. The researchers believe this is because the plastic becomes blended into the asphalt binder. As the road wears over time, the particles that break away are made up of rock, asphalt binder, and polymer together rather than plastic by itself.

The team is also comparing polymer release from the pavement with the amount of tire material found in road dust.

"In our initial Py-GC-MS data," continues Lynch, "we saw tire wear swamps the signal of polyethylene by orders of magnitude, like gigantic peaks! We had to search the weeds of the chromatogram to find signs of polyethylene."

A Possible New Future for Plastic Waste

More testing is still needed to evaluate how well these recycled plastic roads hold up over the long term. Even so, the researchers believe the approach could eventually reduce both landfill waste and marine debris across Hawaii.

"Some people think plastic recycling is a hoax -- that it doesn't work; it's too challenging," Lynch shares. "But this work demonstrates that recycling can work when society prioritizes sustainability."

The research was funded by the Hawaii Department of Transportation.

Meeting

ACS Spring 2026

Title

Harvesting ocean plastics to pave hawaiian roads: Evaluation of microplastic and plastic additive release from asphalt incorporating recycled plastic from various waste streams

Abstract

Polymer modified asphalt (PMA) is used to increase strength and durability of roads. In Hawaii, PMA is typically produced using the virgin co-polymer styrene-butadiene-styrene (SBS). Recycled plastics, such as high-density polyethylene (HDPE), may also be added to asphalt serving to sequester plastic waste. In the state of Hawaii, derelict fishing gear (DFG) is a significant problem, yet it is also a source of HDPE that can be used in recycling. However, asphalt performance and the consequences of adding recycled polymers to asphalt are not well understood. In collaboration with the Hawaii Department of Transportation (HDOT) and the University of Hawaii (UH), the Center for Marine Debris Research (CMDR) are testing the feasibility of using recycled HDPE in asphalt by quantifying microplastics and plastic additives release from roads paved with asphalts made from different combinations of virgin and recycled polymers. The specific asphalt combinations being tested are: SBS (Control-PMA), DFG with and without SBS (DFG-PMA and DFG-neat), Local Waste recycled HDPE with and without SBS (LW-PMA and LW-neat), and Commercially Available, post-industrial recycled HDPE with and without SBS (CA-PMA and CA-neat). Microplastic and plastic additive release under laboratory conditions were performed using a Hamburg Wheel Tracker Test (HWTT) with water sample analyses. Field trials were conducted on a residential road on the island of Oahu, Hawaii. Road dust was swept and analyzed for microplastics by direct analysis and solvent extraction to separate bound plastic from asphalt and plastic additives by water extraction. Microplastic samples utilized pyrolysis gas chromatography mass spectrometry for analysis. Plastic additives are subjected to solid phase extraction with analysis by gas chromatography mass spectrometry. Results produced using these novel analytical methods provide guidance on the use of recycled plastics over virgin plastics in roadways. Moreover, results of this study may provide a viable end of life fate for plastic marine debris, leading to cleaner and healthier oceans.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant environmental innovation where Hawaii researchers are repurposing ocean plastic waste and discarded fishing nets into asphalt for roads. This approach addresses the large-scale problem of marine debris and landfill overflow, with early evidence showing no increased microplastic pollution from these roads. The project has broad environmental impact potential, detailed scientific backing, and could lead to cleaner oceans and reduced plastic waste, making it an inspiring and meaningful environmental breakthrough.
articles.personality_title: Hawaii turns ocean plastic and fishing nets into road pavement
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Hawaii faces a big problem with plastic waste, especially from fishing nets and household plastics. Recycling is hard and costly on the islands, and lots of plastic ends up in the ocean or landfills.

**What happened** – Researchers from Hawaiʻi Pacific University and the Hawaii Department of Transportation started mixing recycled plastics, including fishing nets, into asphalt roads. They paved parts of a street on Oahu with three types of asphalt: one normal, one with recycled household plastic, and one with recycled fishing nets. After nearly a year, they tested the road dust to check if these new roads release more tiny plastic particles than usual.

**Impact** – The tests showed that roads with recycled plastics did not release more microplastics than regular roads. Most tiny particles found came from tire wear, not the plastic in the asphalt. This means recycled plastic roads could be a safe way to reuse ocean waste and reduce landfill overflow without adding pollution.

**What's next step** – Researchers will keep testing these roads to see how well they last over time. If the roads stay strong and safe, this method could be used more widely in Hawaii and other places to turn plastic trash into useful building material.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Hawaii is successfully testing roads made from recycled ocean plastics and fishing nets that do not increase microplastic pollution, offering a new way to reuse waste and protect the environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Hawaï transforme les plastiques océaniques et les filets de pêche en revêtement routier
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Hawaï fait face à un gros problème de déchets plastiques, notamment issus des filets de pêche et des plastiques ménagers. Le recyclage est difficile et coûteux sur les îles, et beaucoup de plastique finit dans l’océan ou les décharges.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université du Pacifique d’Hawaï et du Département des Transports d’Hawaï ont commencé à mélanger des plastiques recyclés, y compris des filets de pêche, dans l’asphalte des routes. Ils ont recouvert une partie d’une rue à Oahu avec trois types d’asphalte : un normal, un avec du plastique ménager recyclé, et un avec des filets de pêche recyclés. Après presque un an, ils ont testé la poussière de la route pour vérifier si ces nouvelles routes libèrent plus de microplastiques que d’habitude.

**Impact** – Les tests ont montré que les routes avec plastiques recyclés ne libèrent pas plus de microplastiques que les routes normales. La plupart des petites particules trouvées provenaient de l’usure des pneus, pas du plastique dans l’asphalte. Cela signifie que les routes en plastique recyclé pourraient être un moyen sûr de réutiliser les déchets océaniques et de réduire les décharges sans ajouter de pollution.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront à tester la durabilité de ces routes sur le long terme. Si elles restent solides et sûres, cette méthode pourrait être utilisée plus largement à Hawaï et ailleurs pour transformer les déchets plastiques en matériaux utiles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Hawaï teste avec succès des routes faites de plastiques océaniques recyclés qui ne génèrent pas plus de microplastiques, offrant un nouveau moyen de réutiliser les déchets et de protéger l’environnement.
articles.personality_title_es: Hawái convierte plásticos oceánicos y redes de pesca en pavimento para carreteras
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hawái tiene un gran problema con los desechos plásticos, especialmente de redes de pesca y plásticos domésticos. Reciclar es difícil y caro en las islas, y mucho plástico termina en el océano o en vertederos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad del Pacífico de Hawái y el Departamento de Transporte de Hawái comenzaron a mezclar plásticos reciclados, incluidas redes de pesca, en el asfalto de las carreteras. Pavimentaron partes de una calle en Oahu con tres tipos de asfalto: uno normal, uno con plástico reciclado de hogares, y otro con redes de pesca recicladas. Después de casi un año, analizaron el polvo de la carretera para ver si estas nuevas carreteras liberan más microplásticos que las normales.

**Impacto** – Las pruebas mostraron que las carreteras con plásticos reciclados no liberan más microplásticos que las normales. La mayoría de las partículas pequeñas provenían del desgaste de los neumáticos, no del plástico en el asfalto. Esto indica que las carreteras con plástico reciclado pueden ser una forma segura de reutilizar desechos del océano y reducir los vertederos sin aumentar la contaminación.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán evaluando cuánto duran estas carreteras. Si se mantienen fuertes y seguras, este método podría usarse más en Hawái y otros lugares para convertir la basura plástica en material útil.

**Resumen en una frase** – Hawái prueba con éxito carreteras hechas con plásticos oceánicos reciclados que no aumentan la contaminación por microplásticos, ofreciendo una nueva forma de reutilizar desechos y proteger el medio ambiente.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a serene Hawaiian coastal scene where discarded fishing nets and plastic debris gently transform into smooth, dark road pavement winding through lush tropical greenery, with subtle textures blending plastic fragments and ocean waves into the asphalt, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: 'Lost' ant species reintroduced to woodland
articles.summary: Southern red wood ants are considered a keystone species, benefiting the ecosystems around them.
articles.content:
A species of ant has been reintroduced to an area of Shropshire as part of a restoration project to support the health of woodlands.

Southern red wood ants are considered a keystone species, meaning they play an important role in supporting healthy ecosystems.

The conservation project is led by the National Trust in partnership with Swansea University and ant conservation specialists Ant Antics, has seen the species moved to woodland near Bridgnorth in an attempt to boost biodiversity across the Dudmaston Estate.

Ewan Chapman, countryside manager for the National Trust, said: "Southern red wood ants play an extraordinary role in woodland ecosystems."

He added: "By bringing them back to Comer Woods, we're restoring natural processes that support healthier soils, richer biodiversity and more resilient woodlands.

"This project is a great example of how targeted conservation action can help rebuild the complex relationships that make these landscapes thrive."
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the successful reintroduction of a keystone ant species to a woodland area, which has a significant positive impact on ecosystem health and biodiversity. The project involves reputable organizations and aims to restore natural processes, benefiting the broader environment and public interest.
articles.personality_title: Lost ant species returns to Shropshire woodland to boost nature
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Southern red wood ants are important insects that help keep forest environments healthy. They are called a keystone species because many other plants and animals depend on them.

**What happened** – A group of these ants was moved back to a woodland area near Bridgnorth in Shropshire. This was done by the National Trust, Swansea University, and a group called Ant Antics. The goal was to help improve the health and variety of life in the Dudmaston Estate woods.

**Impact** – Bringing back the ants helps restore natural processes in the forest. These ants improve soil quality and support many other plants and animals, making the woodland stronger and richer in life.

**What’s next step** – The teams will watch how the ants settle and how the forest changes. If successful, this method could be used in other places to help restore woodlands.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Reintroducing southern red wood ants is helping bring life and balance back to a Shropshire woodland.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une espèce de fourmi disparue revient dans une forêt du Shropshire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les fourmis rouges du sud sont des insectes importants qui aident à maintenir la santé des forêts. Elles sont appelées une espèce clé de voûte car de nombreuses plantes et animaux en dépendent.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un groupe de ces fourmis a été réintroduit dans une forêt près de Bridgnorth, dans le Shropshire. Cette opération a été menée par le National Trust, l'université de Swansea et un groupe appelé Ant Antics. Le but est d'améliorer la santé et la diversité de la vie dans la forêt du domaine de Dudmaston.

**Impact** – Le retour des fourmis aide à restaurer les processus naturels dans la forêt. Ces fourmis améliorent la qualité du sol et soutiennent de nombreuses autres plantes et animaux, rendant la forêt plus forte et plus riche en vie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les équipes vont observer comment les fourmis s'installent et comment la forêt change. Si cela réussit, cette méthode pourra être utilisée dans d'autres endroits pour aider à restaurer les forêts.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La réintroduction des fourmis rouges du sud aide à redonner vie et équilibre à une forêt du Shropshire.
articles.personality_title_es: Una especie perdida de hormiga vuelve a un bosque en Shropshire
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las hormigas rojas del sur son insectos importantes que ayudan a mantener saludables los bosques. Se les llama una especie clave porque muchas plantas y animales dependen de ellas.

**Qué pasó** – Un grupo de estas hormigas fue reintroducido en un bosque cerca de Bridgnorth, en Shropshire. Esto lo hicieron el National Trust, la Universidad de Swansea y un grupo llamado Ant Antics. El objetivo es mejorar la salud y la variedad de vida en el bosque de la finca Dudmaston.

**Impacto** – Traer de nuevo a las hormigas ayuda a restaurar procesos naturales en el bosque. Estas hormigas mejoran la calidad del suelo y apoyan a muchas otras plantas y animales, haciendo que el bosque sea más fuerte y rico en vida.

**Próximo paso** – Los equipos observarán cómo se adaptan las hormigas y cómo cambia el bosque. Si tiene éxito, este método podría usarse en otros lugares para ayudar a restaurar bosques.

**Mensaje en una frase** – Reintroducir las hormigas rojas del sur está ayudando a devolver vida y equilibrio a un bosque en Shropshire.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vibrant woodland floor scene centered on a bustling colony of southern red wood ants building their characteristic large, dome-shaped nest from pine needles and twigs, surrounded by rich soil and diverse plant life, symbolizing the restoration of natural processes and thriving biodiversity in the Dudmaston Estate.

articles.title: Could legal 'personhood' help save Lough Neagh?
articles.summary: Rights of Nature could give Lough Neagh the same status as a person, entitled to the same protections.
articles.content:
Could recognising Lough Neagh as a legal "person" help restore the largest freshwater lake in these islands?

The former lawyer who secured a Rights of Nature recognition for a Spanish saltwater lagoon believes it could.

Prof Eduardo Salazar-Ortuño led the battle to grant the recognition to Mar Menor in Murcia, Spain, after the ecology of the lagoon collapsed in 2016.

He said it could "transform" the future of Lough Neagh.

The lough has been blighted by potentially toxic blue-green algal blooms since 2023, due to pollution primarily from agriculture and wastewater.

Lawyers, activists, fishermen and policy makers came together in Belfast earlier this month to discuss the possibilities.
articles.reasoning: The article discusses a significant environmental initiative to grant legal 'personhood' to Lough Neagh, which could provide strong protections and help restore the largest freshwater lake in the region. This approach has precedent and potential for broad ecological and societal benefits, involving multiple stakeholders and addressing pollution issues.
articles.personality_title: Legal personhood could help protect Lough Neagh from pollution
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Lough Neagh is the largest freshwater lake in the British Isles. Recently, it has suffered from harmful algal blooms caused by pollution from farms and wastewater. These blooms can be toxic and damage the lake’s environment.

**What happened** – A group of lawyers, activists, fishermen, and policymakers met in Belfast to explore giving Lough Neagh legal 'personhood.' This means the lake would have rights similar to a person, allowing stronger legal protections. The idea follows a successful case in Spain, where a lawyer helped a polluted lagoon called Mar Menor gain legal rights after its ecosystem collapsed.

**Impact** – Giving Lough Neagh legal personhood could change how the lake is protected and cared for. It would allow people to take legal action to stop pollution and restore the lake’s health. This approach involves various community members working together to save an important natural resource.

**What's next step** – The group plans to continue discussions and work on legal plans to give Lough Neagh personhood status. If successful, this could lead to new laws that protect the lake and improve its water quality over time.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Granting Lough Neagh legal rights could provide new tools to fight pollution and help restore the lake’s natural health.
articles.personality_title_fr: La personnalité juridique pourrait protéger le Lough Neagh de la pollution
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Lough Neagh est le plus grand lac d'eau douce des îles britanniques. Récemment, il a été touché par des proliférations d'algues toxiques causées par la pollution provenant des fermes et des eaux usées. Ces algues peuvent endommager l'environnement du lac.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un groupe d'avocats, d'activistes, de pêcheurs et de décideurs s'est réuni à Belfast pour envisager de donner au Lough Neagh la personnalité juridique. Cela signifie que le lac aurait des droits similaires à ceux d'une personne, permettant une protection juridique renforcée. Cette idée suit un cas réussi en Espagne, où un avocat a aidé une lagune polluée appelée Mar Menor à obtenir des droits juridiques après l'effondrement de son écosystème.

**Impact** – Donner la personnalité juridique au Lough Neagh pourrait changer la façon dont le lac est protégé et entretenu. Cela permettrait aux gens d'agir en justice pour arrêter la pollution et restaurer la santé du lac. Cette approche réunit différents membres de la communauté pour sauver une ressource naturelle importante.

**Prochaine étape** – Le groupe prévoit de poursuivre les discussions et de travailler sur des plans juridiques pour accorder la personnalité juridique au Lough Neagh. Si cela réussit, cela pourrait conduire à de nouvelles lois protégeant le lac et améliorant la qualité de son eau avec le temps.

**Phrase de conclusion** – Accorder des droits juridiques au Lough Neagh pourrait offrir de nouveaux moyens de lutter contre la pollution et d'aider à restaurer la santé naturelle du lac.
articles.personality_title_es: La personalidad legal podría proteger el Lough Neagh de la contaminación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Lough Neagh es el lago de agua dulce más grande de las islas británicas. Recientemente, ha sufrido floraciones de algas tóxicas causadas por la contaminación de granjas y aguas residuales. Estas algas pueden dañar el entorno del lago.

**Qué pasó** – Un grupo de abogados, activistas, pescadores y responsables políticos se reunió en Belfast para explorar la posibilidad de otorgar a Lough Neagh la 'personalidad legal.' Esto significa que el lago tendría derechos parecidos a los de una persona, permitiendo protecciones legales más fuertes. La idea sigue un caso exitoso en España, donde un abogado ayudó a una laguna contaminada llamada Mar Menor a obtener derechos legales tras el colapso de su ecosistema.

**Impacto** – Darle personalidad legal a Lough Neagh podría cambiar cómo se protege y cuida el lago. Permitirá que la gente tome acciones legales para detener la contaminación y restaurar la salud del lago. Esta idea reúne a diferentes miembros de la comunidad para salvar un recurso natural importante.

**Próximo paso** – El grupo planea continuar las discusiones y trabajar en planes legales para otorgar la personalidad legal a Lough Neagh. Si tiene éxito, podría llevar a nuevas leyes que protejan el lago y mejoren la calidad de su agua con el tiempo.

**Frase clave** – Otorgar derechos legales a Lough Neagh podría ofrecer nuevas herramientas para combatir la contaminación y ayudar a restaurar la salud natural del lago.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vast freshwater lake surrounded by gentle hills, with clear water gradually transitioning from murky to pure, symbolizing restoration; in the foreground, a pair of balanced scales made of intertwined reeds and water, representing legal personhood and protection, while subtle silhouettes of fishing nets and flowing streams connect the lake to the community and nature.

articles.title: Zoo repopulating one of UK's rarest butterflies
articles.summary: Dartmoor Zoo hopes to repopulate rare black-veined white butterflies in the UK.
articles.content:
A zoo is hoping to repopulate a species previously described as extinct in the UK for nearly 100 years.

Dartmoor Zoo said it was working to reintroduce black-veined white butterflies which were believed to have become extinct after World War One, partly due to increased use of pesticides and removal of native hedgerows.

The zoo said nine female butterflies had arrived on Tuesday from Normandy, France, and within 10 minutes of arrival one had already laid a batch of eggs.

Chief executive officer Dr David Gibson said black-veined white butterflies were some of "the rarest animals in the UK at the moment" and the team hoped to change that.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a meaningful conservation effort to reintroduce a butterfly species extinct in the UK for nearly 100 years, which has broad environmental significance and clear positive impact on biodiversity.
articles.personality_title: Dartmoor Zoo begins reintroducing rare black-veined white butterflies to the UK
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The black-veined white butterfly was thought to have disappeared from the UK nearly 100 years ago. Its extinction happened after World War One, mainly because pesticides were used more and native hedgerows where the butterflies lived were removed.

**What happened** – Dartmoor Zoo recently received nine female black-veined white butterflies from Normandy, France. Just minutes after arriving, one of the butterflies laid eggs. The zoo’s team, led by CEO Dr. David Gibson, is now working to raise these butterflies and bring the species back to the UK.

**Impact** – This effort is important because the black-veined white is one of the rarest butterflies in the UK. Bringing it back will help increase biodiversity, which means having more different kinds of plants and animals in nature. It also shows how careful human actions can help repair damage done to wildlife.

**What’s next step** – The zoo will care for the butterflies as they grow and reproduce. If successful, they plan to release the butterflies back into safe natural areas in the UK, helping the species to live in the wild again.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Dartmoor Zoo has started a project to bring back a butterfly species missing from the UK for nearly a century by raising new butterflies from ones brought from France.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le zoo de Dartmoor relance la population du rare papillon blanc à nervures noires au Royaume-Uni
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le papillon blanc à nervures noires était considéré comme disparu du Royaume-Uni depuis près de 100 ans. Son extinction est survenue après la Première Guerre mondiale, principalement à cause de l’usage accru de pesticides et de la disparition des haies naturelles où il vivait.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le zoo de Dartmoor a récemment reçu neuf femelles de ce papillon venant de Normandie, en France. Quelques minutes après leur arrivée, une des femelles a déjà pondu des œufs. L’équipe du zoo, dirigée par le directeur général Dr David Gibson, travaille maintenant à élever ces papillons et à les réintroduire au Royaume-Uni.

**Impact** – Cette initiative est importante car le papillon blanc à nervures noires est l’un des plus rares au Royaume-Uni. Son retour aidera à augmenter la biodiversité, c’est-à-dire la variété des plantes et des animaux dans la nature. Cela montre aussi que des actions humaines réfléchies peuvent réparer les dégâts causés à la faune.

**Prochaine étape** – Le zoo prendra soin des papillons pendant leur croissance et reproduction. Si le projet réussit, ils envisagent de relâcher les papillons dans des zones naturelles protégées du Royaume-Uni pour qu’ils vivent à nouveau dans la nature.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le zoo de Dartmoor a lancé un projet pour faire revenir un papillon disparu du Royaume-Uni depuis presque un siècle en élevant des papillons venus de France.
articles.personality_title_es: El zoológico de Dartmoor comienza a reintroducir la rara mariposa blanca con nervaduras negras en el Reino Unido
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Se creía que la mariposa blanca con nervaduras negras había desaparecido del Reino Unido hace casi 100 años. Su extinción ocurrió después de la Primera Guerra Mundial, principalmente por el aumento del uso de pesticidas y la eliminación de setos nativos donde vivían.

**Qué pasó** – El zoológico de Dartmoor recibió recientemente nueve mariposas hembras de esta especie desde Normandía, Francia. Minutos después de llegar, una ya puso huevos. El equipo del zoológico, dirigido por el director general Dr. David Gibson, trabaja para criar estas mariposas y devolver la especie al Reino Unido.

**Impacto** – Este esfuerzo es importante porque la mariposa blanca con nervaduras negras es una de las más raras en el Reino Unido. Su regreso ayudará a aumentar la biodiversidad, que significa tener más tipos diferentes de plantas y animales en la naturaleza. También muestra cómo las acciones humanas cuidadosas pueden ayudar a reparar el daño hecho a la vida silvestre.

**Próximo paso** – El zoológico cuidará a las mariposas mientras crecen y se reproducen. Si tienen éxito, planean liberar mariposas en áreas naturales seguras del Reino Unido para que la especie vuelva a vivir en libertad.

**Resumen en una frase** – El zoológico de Dartmoor ha iniciado un proyecto para traer de vuelta una mariposa que desapareció del Reino Unido hace casi un siglo, criando mariposas traídas desde Francia.
